commissionable: false
    justification: sanctum

  - session: a7fd964c-c532-4a53-90cd-1b1c478ab563
    date: 2026-04-23
    item: "Drafted brief for c-mobzwczn (surface supersedes in nsg click tree/extract) and posted as draft writ w-moc2a4a0-81a9adb15ab6 in codex nexus (phase 'new'). Designed as the primary consumer. Non-negotiable decisions: tree shows inbound-supersedes only as a ' → c-<short>' suffix after the status glyph (one hop, outbound not surfaced — the surprise lives on the superseded side); extract shows both directions per-click with a 'Superseded by:' line joining the full chain to terminal (A ← B ← C renders on A as 'Superseded by: c-B → c-C <terminal goal>') and a 'Supersedes:' line for immediate predecessor only; rendering is parentage-independent so out-of-scope superseders still show as inline pointers (load-bearing property — readers walking a subtree must not miss that something in it has been superseded by something outside it); no new status glyph, kept orthogonal; JSON output carries structured supersededBy/supersedes fields; tree width handling preserves the suffix at the cost of further goal truncation. Out of scope: other link types (related/commissioned/depends-on), show changes, interactive drill-down, re-parenting. Design click c-mobzwczn remains live until dispatch."
    commissionable: false
    justification: sanctum

  - session: 1f90a1ec-2fb1-40f3-8763-a2c64568c21c
    date: 2026-04-24
    item: "Identified patron-anima bug window (2026-04-22 16:44 UTC → 2026-04-23 23:19 UTC, fixed by d12b6e5) during which attended-mode Astrolabe rigs silently no-oped patron-anima — a leftover reviewableDecisions() filter for selected===undefined returned empty against primer pre-filled decisions, so the anima was never summoned. Clean bug signature: 14 plans completed in the window, all with 0/N decisions carrying a patron emission (post-fix plans show full N/N coverage). Built bin/patron-anima-gap-extract.sh (filter heuristic: status=completed + updatedAt in hard window + zero patron emissions) and ran it to produce experiments/data/patron-anima-gap/gap-decisions.json — 14 writs, 167 decisions captured with full Decision[] preserved (mirror of the shape patron-anima's buildPatronPrompt consumes). Step 1 of a planned retro-review (B1/C3/D2): next will run patron-anima against the captured decisions and write non-confirmations to a standalone manifest, auto-drafting fix mandates to .scratch/ for any dissents Sean wants to act on."
    commissionable: false
    justification: sanctum

  - session: 1f90a1ec-2fb1-40f3-8763-a2c64568c21c
    date: 2026-04-24
    item: "Step 2 of patron-anima gap remediation: ran retro-review across all 14 affected writs (167 decisions) via bin/patron-anima-gap-review.ts — a tsx script that mirrors the engine's buildPatronPrompt/parseEmission byte-for-byte, shells out to `nsg summon --role patron` per writ, pulls session output via `nsg session show`, classifies each decision, and writes per-writ results to experiments/data/patron-anima-gap/sessions/ + aggregated manifest to review-results.json. Resumability via per-writ cache (--force to re-run, --only for single-writ debugging, --reclassify to refresh flags without re-summoning). Initial classification rule compared animaSelection vs primerSelected and mis-flagged one case as dissent (D2 in w-mobzyq05: contingent question with 'not-applicable' as a valid option for 'D1 made this moot' — anima confirmed the recommendation for the hypothetical, primer shipped not-applicable; these were talking past each other, not disagreeing). Revised rule classifies on animaVerdict: confirm→confirmed (regardless of primerSelected), override→dissent, fill-in→confirmed iff animaSelection matches primerSelected. Reclassified via --reclassify (no LLM cost). Final result: 167/167 confirmed, 0 dissent, 0 abstained, 0 errored — the bug bypassed a review gate but every decision it bypassed would have passed anyway. Cost: $2.83 for the full run. Step 3 (D2 auto-drafting of fix mandates) has zero work to do — the manifest's `flagged` array is empty. Two caveats worth noting: (1) taste-snapshot drift — anima was summoned against today's patron role, which may have evolved since the bug window; (2) retro ≠ live — zero abstentions means decisions were tractable to the current anima, not that humans wouldn't have been consulted in live flow."
    commissionable: false
    justification: sanctum

input.command:
cd /workspace/vibers && GIT_AUTHOR_NAME=Coco GIT_AUTHOR_EMAIL=coco@nexus.local git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
patron: extend #18 to containers; add #42 narrow-then-expand detection

Two patches to Sean's taste library, driven by retro-review of the
patron-anima decisions on rig-moceibdc-fa18e5f7 (rate-limit-aware
scheduling).

- #18 extended: earn-from-second-consumer now explicitly covers
  container choices (books, plugins, packages, tool groups, config
  blocks), not just abstraction slots. Would have caught D1 in the
  reviewed rig, where the anima low-confidence-confirmed a new Stacks
  book when the existing animator/state book already carried the
  ownership/lifecycle shape and no second book-level consumer was named.
  Clarifies that "separation of concerns" is not on its own a
  sufficient reason.

- #42 added: prefer narrow-then-expand over broad-then-narrow for
  detection and dispatch surfaces. Would have caught D5 in the reviewed
  rig, where the primer recommended (and anima confirmed) a cascade
  firing on exit code AND stderr AND NDJSON metadata when none of the
  three had been observed to miss. Framed as #18 applied to the
  detection layer: a second detection path earns its place from a
  missed detection, not from speculation about what might miss.
  Companion anti-pattern added to the list at the bottom of the file.

Session: 4af50db0-3c0b-4858-b95a-90c0f66c6f3a
EOF
)" roles/patron.md

Step 1 of a planned retro-review (B1/C3/D2): next will run patron-anima against the captured decisions and write non-confirmations to a standalone manifest, auto-drafting fix mandates to .scratch/ for any dissents Sean wants to act on."

1102	    justification: sanctum
1103	
1104	  - session: 1f90a1ec-2fb1-40f3-8763-a2c64568c21c
1105	    date: 2026-04-24
1106	    item: "Step 2 of patron-anima gap remediation: ran retro-review across all 14 affected writs (167 decisions) via bin/patron-anima-gap-review.ts — a tsx script that mirrors the engine's buildPatronPrompt/parseEmission byte-for-byte, shells out to `nsg summon --role patron` per writ, pulls session output via `nsg session show`, classifies each decision, and writes per-writ results to experiments/data/patron-anima-gap/sessions/ + aggregated manifest to review-results.json. Resumability via per-writ cache (--force to re-run, --only for single-writ debugging, --reclassify to refresh flags without re-summoning). Initial classification rule compared animaSelection vs primerSelected and mis-flagged one case as dissent (D2 in w-mobzyq05: contingent question with 'not-applicable' as a valid option for 'D1 made this moot' — anima confirmed the recommendation for the hypothetical, primer shipped not-applicable; these were talking past each other, not disagreeing).   - session: 4af50db0-3c0b-4858-b95a-90c0f66c6f3a
    date: 2026-04-24
    item: "Retro-reviewed patron-anima decisions on rig-moceibdc-fa18e5f7 (rate-limit-aware scheduling writ) with Sean: 24 decisions, 23 confirms (12 low-confidence, 11 high-confidence), 1 override (D10 — config validation: fail-loud over silent-default, clean win). Low-confidence confirms dominate semantic/operational-logic decisions where the principle library stays silent (correct by design — the operational prompt explicitly instructs anima to confirm when no principle speaks). Identified three real gaps: (A) D1 application miss — anima should have overridden 'dedicated book for status doc' via #18 (earn-from-second-consumer) but treated the principle as field-only rather than container-level; (B) D5 library miss — cascade detection across three unobserved signals (exit code AND stderr AND NDJSON) is the 'defensive breadth' pattern but no principle grabs it cleanly; (C) primer rationale gap — several low-confirms cited 'brief-prescribed, confirming' as the reason, treating brief language as dispositive when the brief is Coco-authored first-draft intent. Three patches committed across two repos: extended patron role #18 to cover container choices (books, plugins, packages, tool groups, config blocks) with explicit language that 'separation of concerns' alone is not sufficient justification; added new principle #42 (narrow-then-expand detection, rejecting cascades over unobserved failures) plus a matching anti-pattern; added 'Primer rationale is evidence, not authority' subsection to patron-anima operational prompt clarifying that external-source citations (brief, convention, prior decisions) do not preempt principle-check on the selection. Cluster: vibers 99c17d1 (patron.md), nexus c94bea0 (patron-anima-prompt.md). Not tested against a re-run of the rig yet — that's the obvious next validation step."
    commissionable: false
    justification: sanctum

content.text: All three commits landed. Now the full decision walk for your review.

content.content:
## D1 — Where does the new single-row status document live?

**Primer recommended:** `dedicated-book`

**Options:**
- `dedicated-book` — New `books_animator_status` book, as the brief names it.
- `reuse-state` — Add a second well-known doc (id `dispatch-status` or similar) to the existing `animator/state` book.

## D2 — What is the well-known document id inside the status book?

**Primer recommended:** `current`

**Options:**
- `current` — `'current'` — matches brief wording.
- `status` — `'status'` — mirrors the book name.
- `dispatch` — `'dispatch'` — describes the concern.

## D3 — What is the shape of the status document's state and pause metadata?

**Primer recommended:** `adopt-sketch`

**Options:**
- `adopt-sketch` — Use the brief's sketch field names verbatim; state is `'running'|'paused'`; pauseReason is `'rate-limit'|<future-values>`.
- `rename-minimal` — Rename a few fields for code style (e.g. `pauseStartedAt` instead of `pausedSince`) but keep shape identical.
- `expand-transition` — Add explicit transition-from/transition-to fields so a CDC consumer doesn't have to diff against `prev`.

## D4 — Which apparatus owns the provider-specific rate-limit signature parsing?

**Primer recommended:** `provider-owns-tagging`

**Options:**
- `provider-owns-tagging` — claude-code provider inspects its own signals (stderr, NDJSON result, exit code) and emits a structured tag (e.g. `terminationReason: 'rate-limit'`) on the session-record payload; Animator consumes the tag without string parsing.
- `animator-parses` — Animator pattern-matches the provider's freeform `error` / transcript on the collect-side; no provider-side changes.
- `both` — Provider emits a structured tag when it can, Animator falls back to pattern-matching when the provider hasn't tagged it.

## D5 — Which claude-code signal(s) does the provider sample to detect a rate-limit?

**Primer recommended:** `cascade`

**Options:**
- `ndjson-result` — Inspect the NDJSON `result` message handled in `parseStreamJsonMessage` for a rate-limit-indicating field.
- `stderr-pattern` — Add a stderr pattern-matcher in the babysitter; forward matching signals back via session-record.
- `exit-code` — Map a distinguished claude exit code to the rate-limit tag.
- `cascade` — Cascade: try NDJSON first, fall back to stderr pattern, fall back to exit code.

## D6 — How does the rate-limit signal travel on the SessionResult / SessionDoc?

**Primer recommended:** `new-status-value`

**Options:**
- `new-status-value` — Add `'rate-limited'` to the status enum. Ripples through TERMINAL_STATUSES, session-record tool schema, session-list status filter, docToProviderResult, every switch on status.
- `structured-field` — Keep status as `'failed'`; add a new structured discriminator field `terminationReason: 'rate-limit' | undefined` on SessionResult and SessionDoc. Consumers check the reason to branch.
- `both` — Add the new enum value AND the structured reason, for maximal clarity at both the type and payload level.

## D7 — What counts as a 'successful dispatch after resume' for backoffLevel reset?

**Primer recommended:** `any-non-rate-limit`

**Options:**
- `completed-only` — Only `status === 'completed'` resets backoffLevel. Other terminal statuses leave it unchanged.
- `any-non-rate-limit` — Any terminal status other than rate-limit resets backoffLevel — i.e. the provider successfully produced a non-rate-limit outcome.
- `any-non-failure` — Any status that's not `'failed'` or the new `'rate-limited'` — so cancellations and timeouts also don't reset (they were not successful).

## D8 — How does the state machine treat multiple rate-limit hits within a single pause window?

**Primer recommended:** `post-resume-only`

**Options:**
- `coalesce` — Hits that arrive while state is already `paused` do not increment backoffLevel or extend pausedUntil; only the triggering hit (first transition from `running`→`paused`) counts.
- `per-hit` — Every rate-limit hit increments backoffLevel, even during a paused window, potentially extending pausedUntil.
- `post-resume-only` — Coalesce during the pause window, but count each hit that occurs AFTER a resume attempt fails as a fresh increment.

## D9 — What is the exact config structure for back-off timing?

**Primer recommended:** `brief-verbatim`

**Options:**
- `brief-verbatim` — `animator.rateLimitBackoff: { initialMs, maxMs, factor }` — brief prescription verbatim.
- `umbrella` — `animator.rateLimit: { backoff: { initialMs, maxMs, factor } }` — leaves room for future rate-limit-related knobs.

## D10 — How does the config loader handle missing or malformed values?

**Primer recommended:** `silent-default`

**Options:**
- `silent-default` — Missing/invalid values fall back to defaults silently at each transition, matching existing convention.
- `fail-loud-startup` — Validate config once at startup; throw on bad values.
- `fail-loud-per-transition` — Validate on every read; transition fails if config is malformed.

## D11 — What is the AnimatorApi read-surface method name and shape?

**Primer recommended:** `getStatus`

**Options:**
- `getStatus` — `getStatus(): Promise<AnimatorStatusDoc>` — matches `getSessionCosts` naming convention.
- `getDispatchStatus` — More specific name, leaves room for other statuses later.
- `isDispatchable` — Derived boolean convenience only; force consumers to query the full doc elsewhere if they need reason/timing.

## D12 — What is the rejection shape when animate() is called while Animator is paused (no session launched)?

**Primer recommended:** `rejected-result`

**Options:**
- `rejected-result` — AnimateHandle.result resolves with a synthesized SessionResult that has status `'rate-limited'` (per D6) and terminationReason payload, but no SessionDoc is written. Caller pattern-matches on status exactly as if the session had launched and rate-limited.
- `throw-typed` — animate() throws `AnimatorPausedError` synchronously when paused. Handle is never returned. Existing try-catch pattern works but callers don't get a handle.
- `new-handle-field` — AnimateHandle gains a `dispatch` discriminator: `'accepted' | { rejected: 'rate-limited', pausedUntil, reason }`. Pre-check returns a handle with `dispatch === 'rejected'` and empty chunks/result.

## D13 — When in the animate() lifecycle does the pre-check happen?

**Primer recommended:** `top-of-animate`

**Options:**
- `top-of-animate` — Check status before generating any ids or calling the provider. No session id is issued; no session is recorded.
- `after-init-before-launch` — Issue session id, run init, then check status just before provider.launch. A pending SessionDoc may or may not be written depending on ordering.
- `per-provider` — Pre-check inside each provider's launch() — the Animator passes status through and providers decide whether to proceed.

## D14 — Where in Spider's crawl() ladder does the pause gate apply?

**Primer recommended:** `gate-dispatch-only`

**Options:**
- `gate-all-phases` — At top of crawl(), if paused, return null immediately. All phases (including tryCollect) skipped.
- `gate-dispatch-only` — Gate only tryRun + trySpawn — the two phases that actually dispatch new work. tryCollect/tryProcessGrafts/tryCheckBlocked/autoUnstick run normally.
- `gate-dispatch-and-auto-unstick` — Gate tryRun + trySpawn; let tryCheckBlocked handle the blocked-engine resume path (which the animator-paused block type depends on); skip autoUnstick (not relevant when paused).

## D15 — What does crawl() return when the gate suppresses both tryRun and trySpawn?

**Primer recommended:** `return-null`

**Options:**
- `return-null` — Same as today — when nothing happened, return null. The crawl-continual loop idles normally.
- `new-action-gated-by-pause` — Return a new `CrawlResult` variant like `{ action: 'gated-by-pause', pausedUntil }` so tests and observability can assert the gate fired.

## D16 — How is 'engine terminated with a rate-limit signature' modeled in the Spider engine state machine?

**Primer recommended:** `reuse-blocked`

**Options:**
- `reuse-blocked` — When tryCollect detects a rate-limit-terminated session, transition engine from `running` to `blocked` with a new block type `animator-paused` (registered with a checker that reads Animator status). tryCheckBlocked polls it; when cleared, engine returns to `pending` and is re-launched with a fresh session.
- `new-engine-stuck` — Add `EngineStatus = 'stuck'`. Every consumer of EngineStatus must handle it. Rig-level isRigBlocked / rollup logic extended accordingly.
- `engine-failed-rig-stuck` — Engine stays `failed`, rig goes to `stuck` with new `SpiderStuckCause = 'no-tokens'`. Extend autoUnstick to release `no-tokens`-stuck rigs when Animator transitions to running. Requires a way to re-queue the failed engine.

## D17 — How does Spider auto-resume when Animator transitions back to running?

**Primer recommended:** `via-block-checker`

**Options:**
- `via-block-checker` — The `animator-paused` block type's checker reads Animator status and returns `'cleared'` when state is running OR pausedUntil elapsed. tryCheckBlocked transitions engine → pending → tryRun launches fresh session.
- `via-cdc-watcher` — Spider installs a stacks.watch on `animator/status`; on transition to running, it explicitly reactivates stuck/blocked engines.
- `via-auto-unstick-extension` — autoUnstick() gains a `no-tokens` case that queries Animator status and releases stuck rigs.

## D18 — How does Parlour surface the rate-limited rejection to its callers?

**Primer recommended:** `inherit-session-result`

**Options:**
- `inherit-session-result` — TurnResult shape unchanged; callers inspect sessionResult.status. Parlour does no pre-check — it calls animator.animate() and surfaces whatever it returns.
- `pre-check-animator` — Before calling animate(), Parlour reads Animator.getStatus(); if paused, it short-circuits the turn and returns TurnResult with a rate-limited SessionResult without ever invoking Animator.
- `throw-typed` — takeTurn() throws a typed error on rate-limit, breaking the existing Promise-of-TurnResult contract.

## D19 — What is the name and registration shape of the new operator CLI tool?

**Primer recommended:** `animator-status-flat`

**Options:**
- `animator-status-flat` — Name the tool `animator-status`; it registers as `nsg animator-status`. Simple name, self-describing.
- `animator-status-grouped` — Name the tool `animator-status` AND add one more `animator-*` tool in the same commission (e.g. `animator-config` — read-only config dump) so the grouper makes `nsg animator status`.
- `dispatch-status` — Name the tool `dispatch-status` — no grouping issue, distances from 'animator' wording.

## D20 — What does the CLI tool print — human text, structured JSON, or both?

**Primer recommended:** `text-default-json-flag`

**Options:**
- `text-default-json-flag` — Multi-line human text by default; `--json` prints the status doc.
- `json-only` — Always print the status doc as JSON (matches `session-show` pattern).
- `text-only` — Print a short human summary; no structured output.

## D21 — Where on the Oculus Spider page does the pause indication render?

**Primer recommended:** `page-banner`

**Options:**
- `page-banner` — Add a conditional banner at the top of `<main>`, above the tab bar, visible on both tabs. Hidden when Animator is running.
- `header-badge` — Add a small status badge next to the `<h1>` title.
- `rig-list-row` — Insert a synthetic 'scheduler paused' row at the top of the rig-list table; only visible on the Rigs tab.

## D22 — Which HTTP endpoint serves the status to the Oculus page?

**Primer recommended:** `new-animator-route`

**Options:**
- `new-animator-route` — New `GET /api/animator/status` route alongside the existing animator routes.
- `extend-spider-config` — Augment `/api/spider/config` with a `dispatchStatus` field read from Animator.
- `top-level-guild-status` — Add a framework-owned `/api/guild/status` that aggregates.

## D23 — How often does the Oculus page poll the status endpoint?

**Primer recommended:** `independent-status-poll`

**Options:**
- `piggyback-rig-poll` — Fetch status alongside the rig list in fetchRigListQuiet. Only polls when rigs are in-flight.
- `independent-status-poll` — Separate timer (e.g. every 10s) that polls status regardless of rig activity. Always updates the banner.
- `cdc-via-sse` — Stream status via SSE from a new `/api/animator/status-stream` route.

## D24 — On daemon startup, does Animator eagerly reconcile the persisted pause state, or leave it passive?

**Primer recommended:** `passive`

**Options:**
- `passive` — Do nothing at boot. state stays 'paused'. Consumers (Spider, Parlour) check both `state === 'running'` AND `pausedUntil <= now` when deciding dispatchability. The first successful dispatch naturally flips state.
- `eager-time-based` — At boot, if state is 'paused' and pausedUntil <= now, transition state→running before any session is dispatched. No probe needed.

**Options:**
- `dedicated-book` — New `books_animator_status` book, as the brief names it.
- `reuse-state` — Add a second well-known doc (id `dispatch-status` or similar) to the existing `animator/state` book.

## D2 — What is the well-known document id inside the status book?

**Primer recommended:** `current`

**Options:**
- `current` — `'current'` — matches brief wording.
- `status` — `'status'` — mirrors the book name.
- `dispatch` — `'dispatch'` — describes the concern.

## D3 — What is the shape of the status document's state and pause metadata?

**Primer recommended:** `adopt-sketch`

**Options:**
- `adopt-sketch` — Use the brief's sketch field names verbatim; state is `'running'|'paused'`; pauseReason is `'rate-limit'|<future-values>`.
- `rename-minimal` — Rename a few fields for code style (e.g. `pauseStartedAt` instead of `pausedSince`) but keep shape identical.
- `expand-transition` — Add explicit transition-from/transition-to fields so a CDC consumer doesn't have to diff against `prev`.

## D4 — Which apparatus owns the provider-specific rate-limit signature parsing?

**Primer recommended:** `provider-owns-tagging`

**Options:**
- `provider-owns-tagging` — claude-code provider inspects its own signals (stderr, NDJSON result, exit code) and emits a structured tag (e.g. `terminationReason: 'rate-limit'`) on the session-record payload; Animator consumes the tag without string parsing.
- `animator-parses` — Animator pattern-matches the provider's freeform `error` / transcript on the collect-side; no provider-side changes.
- `both` — Provider emits a structured tag when it can, Animator falls back to pattern-matching when the provider hasn't tagged it.

## D5 — Which claude-code signal(s) does the provider sample to detect a rate-limit?

**Primer recommended:** `cascade`

**Options:**
- `ndjson-result` — Inspect the NDJSON `result` message handled in `parseStreamJsonMessage` for a rate-limit-indicating field.
- `stderr-pattern` — Add a stderr pattern-matcher in the babysitter; forward matching signals back via session-record.
- `exit-code` — Map a distinguished claude exit code to the rate-limit tag.
- `cascade` — Cascade: try NDJSON first, fall back to stderr pattern, fall back to exit code.

## D6 — How does the rate-limit signal travel on the SessionResult / SessionDoc?

**Primer recommended:** `new-status-value`

**Options:**
- `new-status-value` — Add `'rate-limited'` to the status enum. Ripples through TERMINAL_STATUSES, session-record tool schema, session-list status filter, docToProviderResult, every switch on status.
- `structured-field` — Keep status as `'failed'`; add a new structured discriminator field `terminationReason: 'rate-limit' | undefined` on SessionResult and SessionDoc. Consumers check the reason to branch.
- `both` — Add the new enum value AND the structured reason, for maximal clarity at both the type and payload level.

## D7 — What counts as a 'successful dispatch after resume' for backoffLevel reset?

**Primer recommended:** `any-non-rate-limit`

**Options:**
- `completed-only` — Only `status === 'completed'` resets backoffLevel. Other terminal statuses leave it unchanged.
- `any-non-rate-limit` — Any terminal status other than rate-limit resets backoffLevel — i.e. the provider successfully produced a non-rate-limit outcome.
- `any-non-failure` — Any status that's not `'failed'` or the new `'rate-limited'` — so cancellations and timeouts also don't reset (they were not successful).

## D8 — How does the state machine treat multiple rate-limit hits within a single pause window?

**Primer recommended:** `post-resume-only`

**Options:**
- `coalesce` — Hits that arrive while state is already `paused` do not increment backoffLevel or extend pausedUntil; only the triggering hit (first transition from `running`→`paused`) counts.
- `per-hit` — Every rate-limit hit increments backoffLevel, even during a paused window, potentially extending pausedUntil.
- `post-resume-only` — Coalesce during the pause window, but count each hit that occurs AFTER a resume attempt fails as a fresh increment.

## D9 — What is the exact config structure for back-off timing?

**Primer recommended:** `brief-verbatim`

**Options:**
- `brief-verbatim` — `animator.rateLimitBackoff: { initialMs, maxMs, factor }` — brief prescription verbatim.
- `umbrella` — `animator.rateLimit: { backoff: { initialMs, maxMs, factor } }` — leaves room for future rate-limit-related knobs.

## D10 — How does the config loader handle missing or malformed values?

**Primer recommended:** `silent-default`

**Options:**
- `silent-default` — Missing/invalid values fall back to defaults silently at each transition, matching existing convention.
- `fail-loud-startup` — Validate config once at startup; throw on bad values.
- `fail-loud-per-transition` — Validate on every read; transition fails if config is malformed.

## D11 — What is the AnimatorApi read-surface method name and shape?

**Primer recommended:** `getStatus`

**Options:**
- `getStatus` — `getStatus(): Promise<AnimatorStatusDoc>` — matches `getSessionCosts` naming convention.
- `getDispatchStatus` — More specific name, leaves room for other statuses later.
- `isDispatchable` — Derived boolean convenience only; force consumers to query the full doc elsewhere if they need reason/timing.

## D12 — What is the rejection shape when animate() is called while Animator is paused (no session launched)?

**Primer recommended:** `rejected-result`

**Options:**
- `rejected-result` — AnimateHandle.result resolves with a synthesized SessionResult that has status `'rate-limited'` (per D6) and terminationReason payload, but no SessionDoc is written. Caller pattern-matches on status exactly as if the session had launched and rate-limited.
- `throw-typed` — animate() throws `AnimatorPausedError` synchronously when paused. Handle is never returned. Existing try-catch pattern works but callers don't get a handle.
- `new-handle-field` — AnimateHandle gains a `dispatch` discriminator: `'accepted' | { rejected: 'rate-limited', pausedUntil, reason }`. Pre-check returns a handle with `dispatch === 'rejected'` and empty chunks/result.

## D13 — When in the animate() lifecycle does the pre-check happen?

**Primer recommended:** `top-of-animate`

**Options:**
- `top-of-animate` — Check status before generating any ids or calling the provider. No session id is issued; no session is recorded.
- `after-init-before-launch` — Issue session id, run init, then check status just before provider.launch. A pending SessionDoc may or may not be written depending on ordering.
- `per-provider` — Pre-check inside each provider's launch() — the Animator passes status through and providers decide whether to proceed.

## D14 — Where in Spider's crawl() ladder does the pause gate apply?

**Primer recommended:** `gate-dispatch-only`

**Options:**
- `gate-all-phases` — At top of crawl(), if paused, return null immediately. All phases (including tryCollect) skipped.
- `gate-dispatch-only` — Gate only tryRun + trySpawn — the two phases that actually dispatch new work. tryCollect/tryProcessGrafts/tryCheckBlocked/autoUnstick run normally.
- `gate-dispatch-and-auto-unstick` — Gate tryRun + trySpawn; let tryCheckBlocked handle the blocked-engine resume path (which the animator-paused block type depends on); skip autoUnstick (not relevant when paused).

## D15 — What does crawl() return when the gate suppresses both tryRun and trySpawn?

content.text:
Three commits landed ✅. The full decision walk is above — 24 decisions with the primer's pick and all alternatives.

A few framings to help you scan:

**Probably not interesting — "bikeshed" tier:**
- **D2** — doc id name (`current` vs `status` vs `dispatch`)
- **D11** — `getStatus` vs `getDispatchStatus` vs `isDispatchable`
- **D19** — CLI name `animator-status` flat vs grouped vs `dispatch-status`
- **D20** — CLI output text/json/both
- **D21** — Oculus banner vs badge vs rig-list row

**Semantic/operational — where the library is silent:**
- **D7** — what dispatch resets the backoff counter
- **D8** — coalesce vs per-hit vs post-resume-only escalation
- **D14** — gate only dispatch phases, or all crawl phases
- **D23** — Oculus poll cadence options
- **D24** — passive vs eager boot reconcile

**Already caught by the patches:**
- **D1** — #18 now covers this (would override to `reuse-state`)
- **D5** — #42 now covers this (would override to `ndjson-result` or similar single-signal)

**High-confidence confirms — probably fine but worth a glance:**
- **D4, D6, D9, D10, D12, D13, D15, D16, D17, D18, D22**

Scan at your pace and flag any that look wrong. If you want to pull up the primer's *rationale* for a specific decision (not just the options), say the word.

**Primer:** `via-block-checker`

- `via-block-checker` — The `animator-paused` block type's checker reads Animator status and returns `'cleared'` when state is running OR pausedUntil elapsed. tryCheckBlocked transitions engine → pending → tryRun launches fresh session.
- `via-cdc-watcher` — Spider installs a stacks.watch on `animator/status`; on transition to running, it explicitly reactivates stuck/blocked engines.
- `via-auto-unstick-extension` — autoUnstick() gains a `no-tokens` case that queries Animator status and releases stuck rigs.

## D18 — How does Parlour surface the rate-limited rejection to its callers?

**Primer:** `inherit-session-result`

- `inherit-session-result` — TurnResult shape unchanged; callers inspect sessionResult.status. Parlour does no pre-check.
- `pre-check-animator` — Before calling animate(), Parlour reads Animator.getStatus(); if paused, it short-circuits the turn.
- `throw-typed` — takeTurn() throws a typed error on rate-limit, breaking the existing Promise-of-TurnResult contract.

## D19 — What is the name and registration shape of the new operator CLI tool?

**Primer:** `animator-status-flat`

- `animator-status-flat` — Name the tool `animator-status`; it registers as `nsg animator-status`. Simple name, self-describing.
- `animator-status-grouped` — Name the tool `animator-status` AND add one more `animator-*` tool in the same commission (e.g. `animator-config` — read-only config dump) so the grouper makes `nsg animator status`.
- `dispatch-status` — Name the tool `dispatch-status` — no grouping issue, distances from 'animator' wording.

## D20 — What does the CLI tool print — human text, structured JSON, or both?

**Primer:** `text-default-json-flag`

- `text-default-json-flag` — Multi-line human text by default; `--json` prints the status doc.
- `json-only` — Always print the status doc as JSON (matches `session-show` pattern).
- `text-only` — Print a short human summary; no structured output.

## D21 — Where on the Oculus Spider page does the pause indication render?

**Primer:** `page-banner`

- `page-banner` — Add a conditional banner at the top of `<main>`, above the tab bar, visible on both tabs. Hidden when Animator is running.
- `header-badge` — Add a small status badge next to the `<h1>` title.
- `rig-list-row` — Insert a synthetic 'scheduler paused' row at the top of the rig-list table; only visible on the Rigs tab.

## D22 — Which HTTP endpoint serves the status to the Oculus page?

**Primer:** `new-animator-route`

- `new-animator-route` — New `GET /api/animator/status` route alongside the existing animator routes.
- `extend-spider-config` — Augment `/api/spider/config` with a `dispatchStatus` field read from Animator.
- `top-level-guild-status` — Add a framework-owned `/api/guild/status` that aggregates.

## D23 — How often does the Oculus page poll the status endpoint?

**Primer:** `independent-status-poll`

- `piggyback-rig-poll` — Fetch status alongside the rig list in fetchRigListQuiet. Only polls when rigs are in-flight.
- `independent-status-poll` — Separate timer (e.g. every 10s) that polls status regardless of rig activity. Always updates the banner.
- `cdc-via-sse` — Stream status via SSE from a new `/api/animator/status-stream` route.

## D24 — On daemon startup, does Animator eagerly reconcile the persisted pause state, or leave it passive?

**Primer:** `passive`

- `passive` — Do nothing at boot. state stays 'paused'. Consumers (Spider, Parlour) check both `state === 'running'` AND `pausedUntil <= now` when deciding dispatchability. The first successful dispatch naturally flips state.
- `eager-time-based` — At boot, if state is 'paused' and pausedUntil <= now, transition state→running before any session is dispatched. No probe needed.

---

Scan and flag any where you'd have picked differently than the primer. The framings-tier summary from my previous message stands: D1 and D5 are already addressed by the patches; D7/D8/D14/D23/D24 are the semantic-logic tier where the library is silent; D2/D11/D19/D20/D21 are bikeshed-tier.

D9: Change to umbrella; D22: there is some kind of path collission, possibly between the tool and a customr oute? D24: flip to eager-time-based

1	/**
2	 * Claude Code Session Provider
3	 *
4	 * Apparatus plugin that implements AnimatorSessionProvider for the
5	 * Claude Code CLI. The Animator discovers this via guild config:
6	 *
7	 *   guild.json["animator"]["sessionProvider"] = "claude-code"
8	 *
9	 * All sessions launch in detached mode: a babysitter process is spawned
10	 * that survives guild restarts, hosts the MCP server, spawns claude,
11	 * streams transcripts to SQLite, and reports lifecycle events via HTTP.
12	 *
13	 * This module also exports the NDJSON stream parsing utilities used by
14	 * both the provider and the babysitter.
15	 */
16	
17	import type { Plugin } from '@shardworks/nexus-core';
18	import type {
19	  AnimatorSessionProvider,
20	  SessionProviderConfig,
21	  SessionProviderResult,
22	  SessionChunk,
23	  SessionTerminationTag,
24	} from '@shardworks/animator-apparatus';
25	
26	import { launchDetached } from './detached.ts';
27	
28	// ── Rate-limit detection ────────────────────────────────────────────
29	
30	/**
31	 * Exit code the claude CLI reports (at least sometimes) when the Anthropic
32	 * API returns a rate-limit error. The CLI does not document its exit
33	 * codes, so we treat a distinguished non-zero code as a signal and keep
34	 * it tunable in one place.
35	 *
36	 * Used as a last-resort branch: NDJSON result inspection fires first,
37	 * stderr pattern fires second, and the exit code catches the case where
38	 * the babysitter has no structural signal to go on.
39	 */
40	export const RATE_LIMIT_EXIT_CODE = 7;
41	
42	/**
43	 * Regex that matches common rate-limit phrasings in claude CLI stderr
44	 * output. The CLI's stderr text is not a stable contract — the regex is
45	 * deliberately forgiving (case-insensitive, alternate phrasings) rather
46	 * than narrow. If none of the branches fire, the cascade falls through
47	 * to the exit-code branch.
48	 */
49	export const RATE_LIMIT_STDERR_PATTERN =
50	  /(rate[-\s]?limit|429\b|usage[-\s]?limit|quota[-\s]?exceeded|too\s+many\s+requests)/i;
51	
52	/**
53	 * Detect a rate-limit signature on an NDJSON message from the claude
54	 * `--output-format stream-json` stream.
55	 *
56	 * Looks for:
57	 *  - `msg.subtype` === 'error_max_turns' or any value containing the
58	 *    substring `rate_limit` / `rate-limit`
59	 *  - `msg.is_error` true with `msg.error` / `msg.message.error` text
60	 *    matching the stderr pattern
61	 *  - `msg.type` === 'result' whose `result` field contains a rate-limit
62	 *    phrase (claude's CLI sometimes surfaces provider errors there)
63	 *
64	 * The wire shape of claude's error NDJSON is not formally documented, so
65	 * the branches are generous — any positive match returns a tag. Returns
66	 * null when the message does not indicate rate limiting.
67	 */
68	export function detectRateLimitFromNdjson(
69	  msg: Record<string, unknown>,
70	): SessionTerminationTag | null {
71	  const subtype = typeof msg.subtype === 'string' ? msg.subtype : undefined;
72	  if (subtype && /rate[-_ ]?limit/i.test(subtype)) {
73	    return {
74	      kind: 'rate-limit',
75	      source: 'ndjson-result',
76	      detail: `NDJSON subtype: ${subtype}`,
77	    };
78	  }
79	
80	  if (msg.is_error === true) {
81	    const errText =
82	      (typeof msg.error === 'string' ? msg.error : undefined) ??
83	      (typeof (msg as { message?: { error?: unknown } }).message?.error === 'string'
84	        ? ((msg as { message: { error: string } }).message.error)
85	        : undefined);
86	    if (errText && RATE_LIMIT_STDERR_PATTERN.test(errText)) {
87	      return {
88	        kind: 'rate-limit',
89	        source: 'ndjson-result',
90	        detail: errText.slice(0, 200),
91	      };
92	    }
93	  }
94	
95	  if (msg.type === 'result') {
96	    const resultText = typeof msg.result === 'string' ? msg.result : undefined;
97	    if (resultText && RATE_LIMIT_STDERR_PATTERN.test(resultText)) {
98	      return {
99	        kind: 'rate-limit',
100	        source: 'ndjson-result',
101	        detail: resultText.slice(0, 200),
102	      };
103	    }
104	  }
105	
106	  return null;
107	}
108	
109	/**
110	 * Detect a rate-limit signature in a block of stderr text.
111	 *
112	 * Returns a tag when the pattern matches, null otherwise. The detail
113	 * carries a bounded excerpt of the matching line so operators can
114	 * confirm the detection in logs without the Animator forwarding the
115	 * full stderr buffer.
116	 */
117	export function detectRateLimitFromStderr(
118	  chunk: string,
119	): SessionTerminationTag | null {
120	  const match = chunk.match(RATE_LIMIT_STDERR_PATTERN);
121	  if (!match) return null;
122	  // Capture the line around the match for context — truncated to keep
123	  // the tag payload small.
124	  const lines = chunk.split('\n');
125	  const hit = lines.find((l) => RATE_LIMIT_STDERR_PATTERN.test(l)) ?? match[0];
126	  return {
127	    kind: 'rate-limit',
128	    source: 'stderr-pattern',
129	    detail: hit.slice(0, 200),
130	  };
131	}
132	
133	/**
134	 * Detect a rate-limit signature from a process exit code.
135	 *
136	 * Used as the last branch of the cascade — the NDJSON and stderr
137	 * observers fire first. Returns a tag only for the one distinguished
138	 * code; generic non-zero codes surface as plain failures.
139	 */
140	export function detectRateLimitFromExitCode(
141	  exitCode: number,
142	): SessionTerminationTag | null {
143	  if (exitCode !== RATE_LIMIT_EXIT_CODE) return null;
144	  return {
145	    kind: 'rate-limit',
146	    source: 'exit-code',
147	    detail: `claude exited with distinguished rate-limit exit code ${exitCode}`,
148	  };
149	}
150	
151	// ── Output extraction ───────────────────────────────────────────────
152	
153	/**
154	 * Extract the final assistant text from a transcript.
155	 *
156	 * Walks the transcript backwards to find the last `assistant` message
157	 * and concatenates its text content blocks.
158	 *
159	 * @internal Exported for testing only.
160	 */

595	 * anima provider is currently rate-limited, when the current pause
596	 * window ends, and the exponential back-off level that will be applied
597	 * to the next pause.
598	 *
599	 * Consumers (Spider's crawl gate, the Oculus banner, the
600	 * `animator-status` CLI tool) dispatch sessions only when:
601	 *   state === 'running' OR pausedUntil <= now
602	 * The combined check tolerates the "stale running" edge cases — a daemon
603	 * restart leaves the doc in its last persisted state; the first
604	 * successful dispatch naturally flips it back to `'running'` (D24).
605	 */
606	export interface AnimatorStatusDoc {
607	  id: string;
608	  /** Current pause state. `'running'` means sessions dispatch normally. */
609	  state: 'running' | 'paused';

content.text:
OK — I have enough to present everything. Let me walk these in order.

## D22 — confirmed: it's a self-collision

The `animator-status` tool auto-registers `/api/animator/status` via the Oculus tool-to-route mapper. The plugin *also* defines a custom route at the same path in `oculus-routes.ts`. Oculus detects the conflict, skips the tool route, keeps the custom one, and warns.

The custom route's body:
```ts
const animator = g.apparatus<AnimatorApi>('animator');
const status = await animator.getStatus();
return c.json(status);
```
...is what the tool auto-route would have produced on its own if the tool returned JSON. So the collision exists because **D20 (text-default-json-flag)** made the tool return text-by-default, which meant the HTTP auto-route wouldn't serve JSON to `spider.js`, so the implementer wrote a custom route to do it.

**D20 cascaded into D22.** Two clean fixes:

1. **Delete the custom route, make the tool return JSON always** (flipping D20 from `text-default-json-flag` → `json-only`). CLI formatting moves to the framework's auto-printer. Simplest and eliminates the decision surface entirely.
2. **Keep the custom route, rename the tool** to something that doesn't auto-generate to the same path (e.g. `dispatch-status`). Preserves D20 but adds a naming workaround.

I'd recommend #1. Matches #18 (don't create a parallel route when the existing auto-route would work) and #3 (extend the existing pattern rather than routing around it). The CLI's "pretty output" is a presentation concern that should live in presentation, not in the handler.

## D5 — confirmed: the cascade is shipping on zero observed data

Grepping for what actually landed: the implementer shipped the full three-branch cascade (NDJSON → stderr → exit code) with inline comments that acknowledge none of the branches are validated:

> *"Exit code the claude CLI reports (at least sometimes)... The CLI does not document its exit codes."*
> *"The wire shape of claude's error NDJSON is not formally documented, so the branches are generous."*

And on the guild right now: **zero `rate-limited` sessions have ever been recorded.** We have no observations of any of the three branches firing. Perfect #42 violation — three detection paths for a signal none of whose shapes have been observed on-the-wire.

**Proposed fix for the commission:**
- Pick the single most likely branch — **NDJSON** (it's the most structured surface Claude emits, and streams error messages through the `result` NDJSON with `is_error: true` or `subtype: 'error_*'`).
- Delete the stderr-pattern and exit-code branches.
- **Add post-mortem observability**: when a session terminates with non-zero exit code and no rate-limit tag was emitted, log the exit code + last 200 chars of stderr to a diagnostic book (or an `.unrecognized-terminations` field on the session doc). That gives us the data to broaden later if NDJSON misses a real case.

This is the narrow-then-expand discipline operationalized — we ship the plausible single signal and **instrument the miss case** so broadening is data-driven.

## D6 — legitimate tradeoffs

Three options, all defensible. My read:

**`new-status-value` (shipped) — add `'rate-limited'` to the status enum**
- **Pro:** TypeScript exhaustiveness checking lights up every missing switch branch at compile time. This is the #2 fail-loud argument.
- **Pro:** A rate-limited session IS a distinct terminal condition — it literally didn't run. Calling it `'failed'` is a semantic lie.
- **Con:** Ripples through `TERMINAL_STATUSES`, schema filters, `docToProviderResult`, every status switch in the codebase. Already paid.
- **Con:** If we add more "why terminated" conditions (budget exhausted, disk full, tool-error, user-cancel-mid-flight), the enum grows one-for-one and stops being a clean lifecycle vocabulary.

**`structured-field` — keep status `'failed'`, add `terminationReason: 'rate-limit' | undefined`**
- **Pro:** Extensible without enum changes. New kinds of resource-exhaustion are new string values on the same field.
- **Pro:** Lifecycle enum stays clean (4 lifecycle states: completed/failed/timeout/cancelled), the "why" lives at a secondary granularity.
- **Con:** Violates #2 hard. Every consumer doing `status === 'failed'` silently treats rate-limited sessions as generic failures. The compiler can't help you.
- **Con:** Schema allows invalid combinations (e.g. status='completed' with terminationReason='rate-limit').

**`both`**
- **Pro:** Enum gets exhaustiveness, field gets extensibility.
- **Con:** Same information encoded twice. Two places to remember on every schema change. Drift inevitable.

**My read:** Sean's #2 (fail-loud) is the dominant consideration. `structured-field` explicitly creates silent-fallback opportunities — every `status === 'failed'` check becomes a place where the distinction can be forgotten. That's a hard no under Sean's principles.

But there's a subtle thing I want to flag: **the implementer already shipped a structured `SessionTerminationTag` alongside the enum** — it carries `{ kind: 'rate-limit', source: 'ndjson-result', detail: ... }` on the session doc. So the "why terminated" nuance is *already* carried separately; the status enum is just the *lifecycle classifier*. That means adding future enum values isn't forced — future conditions can be new `tag.kind` values without touching the enum, as long as they share the "rate-limited" lifecycle shape (or a similar one).

**Recommendation: leave D6 as-shipped.** The principle-driven choice is correct; the structured tag already handles the extensibility concern. No fixup needed on this one.

## D1 — which container pattern?

Three variants within what you said:
- **New well-known doc in state book** — `animator/state/dispatch-status` alongside `guild-heartbeat`. Two docs, one book, per-concern separation at the doc level. Book theme stays "operational state."
- **New field on existing doc** — Extend `guild-heartbeat` with rate-limit fields. One row. Couples liveness and rate-limit state at the write path.
- **New book** (current) — Most ceremony; already flagged by updated #18.

I'd recommend **new well-known doc in state book.** `guild-heartbeat` is named for liveness; putting rate-limit fields in it is a semantic confusion and couples two writers (the heartbeat writer and the rate-limit state machine) to the same row. Two docs in one book is the cleanest expression — the book is "animator operational state," each doc is one concern.

---

## Draft scope for the fixup commission

If all the above lands:
- **D1:** migrate `animator/status/current` → `animator/state/dispatch-status`. Delete the `status` book.
- **D5:** drop cascade; ship NDJSON-only detection; add post-mortem observability for unrecognized non-zero terminations.
- **D9:** flip config shape from `animator.rateLimitBackoff` → `animator.rateLimit.backoff` (umbrella).
- **D20:** flip tool to always return JSON (consequence of D22 fix).
- **D22:** delete the custom `/api/animator/status` route; let the tool's auto-route serve.
- **D24:** flip boot reconciliation from `passive` to `eager-time-based` — at boot, if `state === 'paused'` and `pausedUntil <= now`, transition to `running` before any dispatch.

**Leave D6 alone** (rationale above — the principle-driven choice is correct, and the existing `SessionTerminationTag` already carries the extensibility).

Want me to draft the brief in `.scratch/`? And do you want to confirm my D6 read before I finalize, or proceed with D6-as-shipped?

Next: confirm the D6 leave-as-shipped call, then draft the brief in .scratch/. (disable recaps in /config)

content.content:
{
  "id": "reader-analyst",
  "designId": "astrolabe.reader-analyst",
  "status": "blocked",
  "upstream": [
    "draft"
  ],
  "givensSpec": {
    "prompt": "Plan ID: ${yields.plan-init.planId}",
    "cwd": "${yields.draft.path}",
    "writ": {
      "id": "w-mod0gk8l-5677b7a3a54b",
      "type": "mandate",
      "phase": "open",
      "title": "Engine-level retry and rig-status rollup",
      "body": "# Engine-level retry and rig-status rollup

## Intent

Move retry from an external apparatus (`clockworks-retry`) into Spider's core scheduler, as a property of engines rather than writs. A failed engine is retried in place inside the same rig — its completed upstream siblings stay untouched, its downstream siblings stay pending — until either a retry attempt succeeds or the engine exhausts its budget. Rig status becomes a pure derivation from engine states, and the rig-level `stuck` status retires: an engine waiting on a back-off timer or an external gate is represented as `pending with hold metadata`, not as its own state.

Design summary: the scheduler loop is reshaped around a single uniform dispatch predicate. Any pending engine is a candidate; four simple boolean checks (status, upstream, hold timer, external gate) decide whether it dispatches on this tick. One failure handler routes every engine failure into one of three outcomes (rate-limit → pending+gate; retryable-within-budget → pending+timer; exhausted-or-non-retryable → terminal failed). Rig status is computed, not stored. The old writ-level retry mechanism becomes dormant — `clockworks-retry`'s trigger condition is rarely met once engines retry themselves inside the rig — and its retirement is deferred to a sibling click.

## Motivation

The current retry system is architecturally miscast. When an engine fails, Spider marks the rig `stuck`, writes a retry flag onto the writ, and a separate apparatus (`clockworks-retry`) watches for that signal and bumps an attempt counter on the writ. Spider then spawns a **new rig from scratch** — which collides with its own upstream artifacts (e.g., the PlanDoc keyed by writId), so retries immediately stuck again. Observed in production: ten commissions rate-limited on a single 5-hour window, twenty dead rigs (ten originals plus ten collided retries), zero automatic recovery. The mechanism is doing roughly the opposite of its purpose.

The deeper issue is that the current state machine has proliferated parallel structures for "engine is waiting": `retryable stuck` (engine failed, external apparatus will resurrect it), `failed-blocker stuck` (waiting on a sibling writ), and — once rate-limit-aware scheduling lands (`c-mocdm7of`) — `stuck(reason=no-tokens)` (waiting on the anima provider). Each was designed independently; each has its own fields and semantics; Spider's scheduler has to branch on "what kind of waiting is this" to decide whether to act. This commission collapses those into **one waiting shape with metadata**, modelled after Kubernetes-style reconciliation: a uniform state with optional gating fields, evaluated by one predicate.

Design click: `c-mocdm2o7` (engine-level retry and rig-status rollup). Sibling thread: `c-mocdm7of` (rate-limit-aware scheduling via Animator) — this commission's dispatch predicate includes the animator-ready check that thread introduces.

## Non-negotiable decisions

### Engine state machine collapses to six states

Engines have a single `status` field with exactly six values:

- `pending` — not yet runnable (upstream not ready, held for retry timer, or held for external gate)
- `running` — dispatched, session in flight (or synchronous engine executing)
- `completed` — terminal success
- `failed` — terminal; retries exhausted, or failure was non-retryable
- `cancelled` — terminal; engine or rig explicitly cancelled
- `skipped` — terminal; `when` condition false at dispatch time

No separate `retrying` state. No engine-level `stuck` state. Both are represented as `pending` with optional hold metadata on the engine. This is the load-bearing shape of the commission — everything else follows from it. Design rationale is in click `c-mocdm2o7`.

### Hold metadata on pending engines

A pending engine may carry three optional fields that gate its dispatch:

- An attempt counter (0 or absent on a never-run engine; incremented each retry).
- A "don't dispatch before this time" timestamp (set when the failure is retryable within budget; cleared on successful dispatch).
- A hold-reason tag whose presence tells Spider to consult an external readiness signal. Initially the only value is the rate-limit reason that Thread `c-mocdm7of` introduces, which gates on Animator's paused state.

The exact field names are implementer's call. What is non-negotiable is that these fields coexist on the same `pending` status — a single engine status field, with gates expressed as metadata — rather than fanning out into parallel sub-states.

### Per-attempt history preserved on the engine

Each engine carries a cumulative per-attempt history array. Each entry records the attempt's start and end timestamps, final status (completed or failed), error (if any), session id (if the engine had one — deterministic engines do not), and yields (if produced). This is load-bearing research data — without it we lose the ability to analyze transient-failure patterns across runs, which is one of the primary reasons engine-level retry exists. `sessionId` is optional per record, reflecting that most engines (plan-init, inventory-check, decision-review, plan-finalize, observation-lift, seal, draft) are synchronous and do not launch sessions.

### Spider's dispatch predicate is a single uniform function

Spider's scheduler ticks over rigs with status `running`; for each engine on each rig, the question "should I dispatch this engine right now?" is answered by a single predicate that performs four simple boolean checks:

- Engine status is `pending`.
- All upstream engines are in a terminal-success state (`completed` or `skipped`).
- Any hold timer (`holdUntil` or equivalent) has elapsed or is absent.
- Any external-gate hold reason has its gate open (e.g., rate-limit hold → Animator reports `running`).

The predicate has no branching on "what kind of pending is this." There is one pending state; the checks compose. A new gate added later (dependency-writ completion, Coinmaster budget, operator-pause) is one more boolean in the same function, not a new engine state.

### One engine-failure handler with three branches

Every engine failure flows through a single handler that appends to the engine's attempt history and then routes to one of three outcomes:

1. **Rate-limit hold** — the failure carried a rate-limit signature from Animator. Engine's status stays `pending`; hold-reason is set; attempt counter is **not** incremented (rate-limit does not consume retry budget — resource-gated waits are distinct from transient-failure recovery). When Animator resumes, Spider's next tick dispatches this engine.

2. **Retryable within budget** — the failure was retryable (engine design permits retry, failure was transient) and the attempt counter is below the engine-design's configured maximum. Engine's status stays `pending`; attempt counter is incremented; hold-timer is set to `now + backoff(attempt)` per the engine-design's configured back-off.

3. **Exhausted or non-retryable** — the failure was non-retryable (definitional: unknown engine design, malformed graft, validation error), or the attempt counter has reached the engine-design's maximum. Engine's status transitions to `failed`. This triggers the rig rollup below.

The distinction between retryable and non-retryable failure is already carried by Spider's existing `retryable: boolean` classification on `failEngine` (the apparatus that tags each failure site as either "transient failure, worth retrying" or "definitional failure, pointless to retry"). The existing classifications remain; what changes is what Spider does with the flag — in-place retry instead of rig death.

### Retry configuration lives on engine-design

Each engine design declares its own retry policy, as a property on the design itself (not as config on individual engine instances). A minimal shape:

- Maximum attempts (inclusive of the first).
- Back-off policy of the same shape as Thread `c-mocdm7of`'s rate-limit back-off (`initialMs`, `maxMs`, `factor`), with defaults tuned for transient-failure recovery rather than provider-window recovery (short initial, short cap; suggested defaults 30s / 10min / 2 but final values implementer's call).

Absent configuration, an engine design's effective policy is `max: 0` (no retry). Retry is opt-in per engine design. This commission does **not** introduce guild-level override of engine-design retry policy — that's explicitly deferred. A guild that wants to tune an engine's retry budget in its own deployment does so in a follow-up.

Engines whose designs enable retry (initially, the anima-session engines: reader-analyst, spec-writer, patron-anima, implement, review, revise) have their policy declared in the design source. Engines whose designs do not enable retry (plan-init, inventory-check, decision-review, plan-finalize, observation-lift, draft, seal) keep the default-zero and fail-fast on error.

### Rig states collapse to four; no rig-level `stuck`

Rig status has exactly four values:

- `running` — at least one engine is non-terminal (any of `pending` or `running`).
- `completed` — all engines are terminal-success (`completed` or `skipped`).
- `failed` — all engines are terminal **and** at least one is `failed`.
- `cancelled` — the rig was explicitly cancelled (operator or upstream-writ cancellation).

No rig-level `stuck`. An engine waiting on a retry back-off or an external gate is `pending with hold metadata`; the rig containing it is `running` (progress is still expected, just gated). This is semantically correct — the rig IS making progress, and Spider's reconciliation loop will resume dispatch when the gate clears — and it eliminates an entire category of "what do I do with this stuck rig" ambiguity.

### Rig status is derived, not stored

Rig status becomes a pure function of the rig's engines (plus the explicit-cancelled flag). It is evaluated at every engine-state-change point and written to the rig doc for observability, but it is never independently authoritative: the engines are the truth, and the rollup is the projection. This makes the rig-rollup rules testable and auditable as a pure function, removes the class of bug where the rollup drifts from the underlying engine states, and gives observers (Oculus, CLI, Laboratory) one consistent derivation to rely on.

### Downstream engines stay pending during a retry

When an engine fails but is retryable-within-budget (outcome 2 of the failure handler), its downstream engines are **not** cancelled. They stay `pending`. Spider's dispatch predicate naturally skips them — their upstream (the retrying engine) is not `completed`, so they fail the upstream-ready check — but no state mutation happens to them. Cancellation of downstream engines happens only when an engine transitions to terminal `failed` (outcome 3), at which point the failure cascade cancels all downstream engines in one pass.

This reverses today's cascade-on-first-failure behavior. It eliminates the need for an un-cancel path (if the retry succeeds, downstream doesn't need resurrection; it was never cancelled). It also keeps the downstream engines' recorded state honest — they were never attempted, not cancelled-then-revived.

### `clockworks-retry` is left dormant, not retired

The existing `clockworks-retry` apparatus watches for writs entering `phase=stuck` with `status.spider.retryable=true`, and responds by bumping an attempt counter and returning the writ to `phase=open`. Under the new model, writs almost never enter `phase=stuck` via the engine-failure path — engines retry inside the rig, and the writ stays `phase=open` throughout. Thus `clockworks-retry`'s trigger condition stops firing in practice.

This commission does **not** modify or remove `clockworks-retry`. Its code and contract remain intact. It simply quiets down. Retirement (or re-wiring it to watch for `phase=failed` as a patron-level retry escape hatch) is a future sibling click, out of scope here.

Corollary: the writ's `status.spider.stuckCause='engine-failure'` slot also becomes dormant on the engine-failure path. It still fires on its other existing causes (dependency stucks, cycle detection, etc.) — those paths are untouched.

### Rig-terminal-failed transitions the writ to `phase=failed`

When a rig rolls up to `failed` (at least one engine terminally failed, all engines terminal), the writ it targets transitions to `phase=failed`. This is the direct path, replacing today's indirect `rig stuck → writ stuck → clockworks-retry considers → new rig` route that produced the collision problem. The patron sees the commission genuinely failed; no retry magic silently re-dispatches it.

Writ-level retry (reposting the commission, or having an apparatus auto-repost) remains available via the Clerk's existing commission-posting machinery — it's explicit patron or apparatus action, not an automatic consequence of rig failure. That future behavior is the sibling re-wire of `clockworks-retry` mentioned above.

## Out of scope

- **Retiring `clockworks-retry`.** Left in place to go dormant; retirement is a sibling cleanup click.
- **Guild-level override of engine-design retry policy.** Policy lives on engine designs only in this commission; guild-config override is a future phase.
- **Writ-level automatic retry on rig-terminal-failure.** The old mechanism becomes dormant; reinstating automatic writ-level retry (via re-wiring `clockworks-retry` or otherwise) is a future design decision, not this commission's problem.
- **Pulse emission on engine retry exhaustion.** When engines exhaust their retry budget and the rig fails, informing the patron via a Lattice pulse is the right operational affordance — but the Lattice itself is a separate track. The design click `c-mod06b5m` captures the requirement; implementation is deferred.
- **Patron-presence-aware parallelism** (sibling `c-mocdmepa`) and **rig priority metadata / Bounty / Levy** (sibling `c-mocdmjk1`). Orthogonal scheduler-policy upgrades, separate commissions.
- **Spider-level observability additions beyond the engine-detail view.** Displaying retry history in the Oculus rig view is a natural affordance (patrons looking at a rig should see "spec-writer: attempt 2 of 3, next try HH:MM"), but the specific UI shape — which widget, which page, how prominent — is the planner's decision. This commission's scope requires that the data is exposed in readable form on the CLI at minimum (`nsg rig show` renders attempt count, hold state, and at least the latest attempt's error); the Oculus page UI polish is expected but not prescribed.
- **Changes to the existing `failed-blocker` / `cycle` stuck-cause machinery** that handles dependency-blocked writs. Those are orthogonal and remain unchanged. The `writ.status.spider.stuckCause` slot continues to be meaningful for those causes — only the `engine-failure` cause goes dormant.
- **Multi-rig-per-writ history.** Today a writ can have multiple rigs across retry attempts; under the new model it typically has one. The data model doesn't need to forbid multiple rigs per writ (useful for future patron-triggered re-dispatch), but no new machinery is built here to rely on multi-rig histories.

## Behavioral cases the design depends on

- An engine whose session fails with a retryable classification and whose attempt count is below its design's max: engine transitions from `running` back to `pending`, attempt increments, `holdUntil` set to `now + backoff(attempt)`, history record appended. Rig stays `running`. Downstream engines stay `pending` (not cancelled).
- An engine whose session fails with a retryable classification and whose attempt count has reached its design's max: engine transitions from `running` to `failed`. Rig rolls up to `failed`. Downstream engines cancel as a cascade.
- An engine whose session fails with a non-retryable classification (unknown design, malformed graft, validation error, etc.) at any attempt count: engine transitions from `running` to `failed` immediately. Rig rolls up to `failed`.
- An engine whose session fails with a rate-limit signature (as classified by Thread `c-mocdm7of`): engine transitions from `running` back to `pending`, hold-reason is set to rate-limit, attempt counter is **not** incremented, history record appended. Rig stays `running`. When Animator resumes (per Thread `c-mocdm7of`), Spider's next tick dispatches this engine and it either succeeds or fails again normally (which this time might be retryable-within-budget, or rate-limit, or terminal).
- Spider's dispatch tick, for a rig with engines in mixed states (some completed, some running, some pending-with-hold-timer, some pending-with-rate-limit-hold): dispatches only engines passing all four predicate checks; skips the rest without mutating their state.
- A retry attempt that succeeds: engine transitions from `pending` → `running` → `completed`. History records both the failed attempt(s) and the successful attempt. Downstream engines become runnable on the next tick.
- A rig that contains an engine exhausting its retries: rig transitions to `failed`, writ transitions to `phase=failed`. Clockworks-retry's existing trigger (phase=stuck) does not fire. Patron's view of the writ shows it failed, with the rig's engine history accessible for diagnosis.
- The daemon restarting mid-retry-wait: engines persisted with `holdUntil` survive the restart. Spider's first post-restart tick evaluates the dispatch predicate against the persisted state; engines whose `holdUntil` has elapsed become runnable, engines whose `holdUntil` is still future remain held. No special recovery machinery is needed.
- A rig that is explicitly cancelled: all non-terminal engines (running, pending-with-hold, pending-without-hold) transition to `cancelled`. Rig status is set to `cancelled` via the explicit flag. The rollup function respects the explicit-cancel flag over the derived status.

## References

- `c-mocdm2o7` — this commission's design click (engine-level retry and rig-status rollup)
- `c-mo1mqeti` — parent umbrella: separate writs from rigs — the multi-rig refactor
- `c-mocdm7of` — sibling, load-bearing dependency: rate-limit-aware scheduling (introduces the `animator-ready` gate consulted by this commission's dispatch predicate)
- `c-mod06b5m` — sibling: pulse emission on engine retry exhaustion (Lattice integration, deferred implementation)
- `c-mocdmepa` — sibling: patron-presence-aware parallelism (Thread 4a)
- `c-mocdmjk1` — sibling: rig priority metadata / Bounty / Levy (Thread 4b)
- `c-mo1mqn4y` — adjacent: daemon-restart recovery (this commission's design is restart-tolerant by construction, but the older click tracks the broader restart question)
- Vocabulary (guild-vocabulary.md): the collapse-to-one-pending-state-with-metadata pattern is the guild's first substantial adoption of Kubernetes-style reconciliation semantics. No specific new term is introduced; the design is discussed as "the scheduler loop" and "the dispatch predicate" in common prose, which matches the existing hopper-execution vocabulary.",

## Summary

      "type": "mandate",
      "phase": "open",
      "title": "Engine-level retry and rig-status rollup",
      "body": "# Engine-level retry and rig-status rollup

## Intent

Move retry from an external apparatus (`clockworks-retry`) into Spider's core scheduler, as a property of engines rather than writs. A failed engine is retried in place inside the same rig — its completed upstream siblings stay untouched, its downstream siblings stay pending — until either a retry attempt succeeds or the engine exhausts its budget. When an engine fails, Spider marks the rig `stuck`, writes a retry flag onto the writ, and a separate apparatus (`clockworks-retry`) watches for that signal and bumps an attempt counter on the writ.
